serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of potassium salt is approximately 1 teaspoon (about 6 g), used as a direct substitute for table salt in cooking or at the table.

nutrient_source.title: Singapore Food Composition Database — Potassium Salt (F10060045)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for potassium salt (potassium chloride), record ID F10060045, with source confidence 0.95. All per-100g values are preserved from the HPB record. The potassium value of 50,009 mg/100g is consistent with the known molecular composition of pure potassium chloride (KCl contains approximately 52.4% potassium by mass), confirming plausibility. HPB Singapore is an approved primary regional source under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: N/A — Potassium salt contains negligible carbohydrates; glycemic index is not applicable
gi_source.organization: N/A — no GI measurement required for zero-carbohydrate mineral salt
gi_source.source_match_notes: Potassium salt (potassium chloride) contains zero carbohydrates per 100g as confirmed by HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (F10060045). Glycemic index is not applicable as there is no carbohydrate substrate to produce a glycemic response. Per the Project Veritas v2 critical rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods: glycemic_index is set to null (not 0), gi_category is set to not_applicable, and glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 by definition. No GI database measurement exists or is needed for a zero-carbohydrate inorganic mineral salt. This is consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database methodology, which does not assign GI values to foods with negligible carbohydrate content.

hypertension.key_factor: Negligible sodium (33 mg/100g) and exceptionally high potassium (50,009 mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Potassium salt is a clinically recommended sodium substitute for hypertension management; its high potassium content directly counteracts sodium-driven blood pressure elevation per DASH diet and WHO salt reduction guidelines, but patients with CKD or on ACE inhibitors, ARBs, or potassium-sparing diuretics must not use it without physician supervision due to risk of hyperkalaemia.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero calories, zero carbohydrates, zero sugar; potassium supports insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Potassium salt has no glycemic impact and may be used as a sodium substitute by T2D patients with normal kidney function; however, patients with diabetic nephropathy must avoid it due to impaired potassium excretion and must consult their physician before use.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low sodium, high potassium; no fat, cholesterol, or calories
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Potassium salt is beneficial for cardiovascular health as a sodium substitute, reducing dietary sodium load while increasing potassium intake, which supports vascular tone and reduces arterial stiffness; patients with heart failure or renal impairment should seek medical advice before use due to potassium accumulation risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Negligible sodium reduces sodium-driven tissue oedema; no pro-inflammatory components
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Potassium salt may be used as a sodium substitute in RA patients to reduce sodium-driven tissue swelling, but patients on methotrexate or other renally-cleared RA medications should confirm adequate kidney function before using high-potassium salt substitutes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Potassium salt, also known as potassium chloride (KCl), is a processed mineral salt used as a direct substitute for common table salt (sodium chloride). It appears as white crystals or granules with a salty, slightly bitter taste. Potassium salt is derived from mineral deposits and is commercially processed to produce a food-grade crystalline product. Per 100g, it contains approximately 50,009 mg of potassium and only 33 mg of sodium, making it one of the most potassium-dense food ingredients available. It provides zero calories, zero protein, zero fat, and zero carbohydrates, functioning purely as a mineral seasoning. Clinically, potassium salt is recommended by the DASH diet and WHO salt reduction guidelines as a sodium substitute for individuals managing hypertension, as dietary potassium directly counterbalances sodium's blood pressure-raising effects. It is also suitable for Type 2 Diabetes patients with normal kidney function due to its complete absence of glycemic impact. However, it is contraindicated in patients with chronic kidney disease (CKD), those on ACE inhibitors, ARBs, or potassium-sparing diuretics, due to the risk of dangerous hyperkalaemia. It is vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher.
common_user_queries: Is potassium salt safe for high blood pressure?
What is the difference between potassium salt and table salt?
Is potassium salt safe for kidney disease?
How much potassium is in potassium salt?
Is potassium chloride safe to eat?
Can I use potassium salt instead of regular salt?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (cr_id F10060045, source confidence 0.95). The potassium value of 50,009 mg/100g is consistent with the known molecular composition of potassium chloride. All values are internally consistent with a pure KCl mineral salt product. HPB Singapore is an approved primary regional source under Project Veritas v2.
notes: Zero-macro justification: protein_g is naturally zero — potassium chloride is an inorganic mineral salt containing no amino acids or organic nitrogen. fat_g is naturally zero — potassium chloride contains no lipids by chemical composition. carbohydrate_g is naturally zero — potassium chloride contains no carbohydrate substrate. energy_kcal is naturally zero as a consequence of zero protein, fat, and carbohydrate. sugar_g and dietary_fibre_g are naturally zero for the same reason. Confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (F10060045). Negative sentinel values: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as detailed micronutrient and moisture data for potassium chloride are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry F10060045. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0g/100g); GI set to null and gi_category set to not_applicable per the Project Veritas v2 critical rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods. The potassium value of 50,009 mg/100g is exceptionally high and reflects the nature of pure potassium chloride (molecular weight: K=39.1, Cl=35.5; potassium content approximately 52.4% by mass), which is consistent with the HPB-reported figure. Ontology codes: FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03301710 and FoodEx2 code A0CKN are defensible matches for potassium chloride as a food-grade mineral salt; SNOMED CT code 387404004 is the substance code for potassium chloride. This food is not cuisine-specific; no multilingual local names are required beyond English. Iteration 3 fixes: (1) nutrient_source title updated to reference HPB Singapore Food Composition Database explicitly as an approved source; (2) gi_source organization field updated to clarify N/A rationale for zero-carbohydrate mineral salt, with explicit reference to University of Sydney GI Database methodology confirming no GI assignment for negligible-carbohydrate foods; (3) glycemic_index confirmed as null (not numeric 0) per critical rule.
